coredata.dc:description: Determining and analyzing the precise location where drugs and coformers interact is essential for engineering customized drug delivery systems. The carbon dot (CD) was derived from the plant material and experimentally (UV-Vis, emission, FTIR and TEM) confirmed. The deep understanding of UV-Vis and FTIR data was achieved by the DFT (r2SCAN-3c) method, and the theoretically derived structure of CD was used to analyze the interaction between CD and amantadine (AM). The interaction between CD and AM was examined thoroughly. Highly accurate and reliable HFLD, SAPT0 and XEDA were applied. The hydrogen transfer pathway was drown using potential energy surface calculation using r2SCAN-3c. The Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM), Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP), Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) and Independent Gradient Model Plot (IGMplot) study were utilized to investigate the interactive site. The findings of this investigation will prove valuable for creating innovative and adaptable drug delivery platforms that leverage carbon dot technology.

head.ce:title: Molecular-scale insights into hydrogen bond interaction and proton transfer on carbon dot and amantadine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determining and analyzing the precise location where drugs and coformers interact is essential for engineering customized drug delivery systems. The carbon dot (CD) was derived from the plant material and experimentally (UV-Vis, emission, FTIR and TEM) confirmed. The deep understanding of UV-Vis and FTIR data was achieved by the DFT (r2SCAN-3c) method, and the theoretically derived structure of CD was used to analyze the interaction between CD and amantadine (AM). The interaction between CD and AM was examined thoroughly. Highly accurate and reliable HFLD, SAPT0 and XEDA were applied. The hydrogen transfer pathway was drown using potential energy surface calculation using r2SCAN-3c. The Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM), Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP), Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) and Independent Gradient Model Plot (IGMplot) study were utilized to investigate the interactive site. The findings of this investigation will prove valuable for creating innovative and adaptable drug delivery platforms that leverage carbon dot technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots represent a new class of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, characterized by discrete, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with diameters typically below 10 nm. They were first discovered in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes via preparative electrophoresis [1–4] CD show unique properties such as high thermal [5] and photostability [6], low toxicity [7,8], good water solubility [9–11], high biocompatibility [12,13], and eco-friendly nature [14,15]. CD can inherit special optical properties such as photoluminescence, electrochemiluminescence, and chemiluminescence owing to their quantum confinement and edge effects [3,16]. CD are inexpensive and abundant; therefore, they are a potential alternative to semiconductor quantum dots. The unique optical, chemical, and biological characteristics of these substances have led to their extensive use in various fields, including bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, and solar energy devices [12,17,18]. Since the discovery of carbon dot, many researchers have focused on their production using natural resources, including orange waste peel, willow leaves, and sugarcane wastes [6,13,19–21]. CD synthesized from natural resources are advantageous because of their sustainability, cost-effectiveness, and bio-compatibility [22]. The use of biomass as a precursor not only leverages the inherent heteroatom content for doping but also aligns with green chemistry principles [23,24]. These benefits make CD from natural sources particularly suitable for a range of applications, including bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, and environmental monitoring [2]. Through numerous chemical interactions, CD may also be used to detect pharmaceutical chemicals in water, including antibiotics, anticancer medications, antiseptic drugs, anaesthetic drugs, vitamins, neurotransmitters, and hypnotic drugs [25].

Over the years, several experimental and theoretical approaches have been employed to elucidate the structural and electronic properties of CD. Techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD) have been instrumental in determining crystalline and phase composition [26], while Raman spectroscopy provides insights into domains and defect structures [27]. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy provides detailed information about the surface functionalities and chemical environment of carbon dots [28]. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy has been extensively used to detect paramagnetic species and defect centers in carbon dots, offering critical insights into their surface states and electronic structure [29]. Furthermore, secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS) offers high-resolution spatial and elemental information about the core and surface functionalities [30]. In parallel, density functional theory (DFT) and other computational modeling approaches have significantly advanced the theoretical understanding of CDs. These studies help predict electronic structure, band gaps, charge distribution, and interaction energies with guest molecules or solvents, complementing experimental observations [31]. For instance, DFT-based analyses of HOMO-LUMO energy gaps, NCI and QTAIM provide molecular-level insights into the reactivity and stability of CDs [32].

The carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine functional groups that adorn the surface CD can engage in hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, coordination bonds, and non-covalent interactions with a variety of molecules [33]. Functionalized carbon dot represents a significant advancement in drug delivery systems, offering biocompatibility and dual functionality for drug carriage and cellular imaging [34]. Their small size, facilitates cellular uptake and allows for the rational design of biocompatible drug carriers, presenting a superior alternative to traditional organic dyes and imaging contrast agents [35,36]. The unique functionalized surface properties of CD make them suitable for various applications, particularly drug delivery. Surface modified CD with drugs can effectively prevent and treat cancer cells. In this regard, the drug delivery capability of boldine-loaded CD was investigated as drug carriers for targeted human breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells) [37]. Moreover, folic acid and carbon dots (FA-CD) have low toxicity and strong biocompatibility, making them safe for use in in vivo research and a viable tool for targeted cancer diagnostics and therapy [38]. According to earlier studies, CD derived from 4-aminophenyl boronic acid hydrochloride could significantly lower the amount of Herpes Simplex Virus that entered human lung cancer cells and monkey kidney cancer cells during in vitro testing [39].

CD presents a promising platform for drug delivery systems that can enhance therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing adverse reactions. Their versatility and exceptional properties make them well-suited for the development of cutting-edge drug delivery systems [40,41]. CD are useful FRET pair donors and drug carriers, eliminating the need for additional fluorescence labeling. Furthermore, the stable fluorescence and efficient FRET between CD and the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) allow for convenient cell imaging and real-time monitoring of drug release [42].

Amantadine is a versatile medication that is widely available and is derived from adamantine, a tricyclic water-soluble amine derivative [43]. It is not only used as an antiviral agent but also as a tool for studying the mechanisms of viruses penetration and as an aid in the classification of virus [44,45]. It is also used as an antidyskinetic drug, primarily for Parkinson's disease and Parkinson's syndromes. It was then found to exhibit anti-glycoxidative properties that protect cells and tissues from oxidative stress and harmful glycoxidation [46]. In addition, it has been studied for its potential use in the treatment of traumatic brain injury, multiple sclerosis, chronic fatigue syndrome, and depression [47,48]. In addition, amantadine showed activity against HHV-6B -infected MOLT-3 cells [49]. A recent study has revealed that amantadine infusion is beneficial for painful peripheral diabetic neuropathy [50]. Actually, it was discovered as the first inhibitor of the influenza virus, which works by blocking the M2 protein ion channel of the virus and preventing the early stages of replication [43]. Unfortunately, its effectiveness has declined in recent years due to the emergence of influenza strains that have developed resistance to amantadine [51].

Recent studies have indicated that combining amantadine with coformers enhances its activity and impact [52–54]. However, their presence in cocrystals can have adverse effects. When sulfamethoxazole is combined with other pharmaceutical agents (such as amantadine), there is a risk of unforeseen drug-drug interactions that could alter the pharmacokinetics or pharmacodynamics of the drugs involved. This can lead to reduced efficacy and increased toxicity [54]. Resveratrol (RVA) exhibits low solubility, which may present a challenge for effective cocrystal formation. This limited solubility could restrict the amount of RVA that can be integrated into the cocrystal, potentially impacting its overall efficacy [52]. Additionally, basic drugs with acidic coformers, can potentially decrease drug solubility and bioavailability compared to the free drug form, negating the intended benefits of cocrystallization [55]. Moreover, the dissociation of co-crystal can unravel the benefits of co-crystal formation, leading to negative impacts on the product performance. These effects include changes in dissolution rate and bioavailability [56,57]. CD is a suitable option as a coformer in drug delivery systems and can adequately address the majority of the issues discussed above. Previous theoretical research has examined complexes that arise between carbon dots and various drugs [58,59]. Currently, there is no experimentally validated carbon dot system for examining interactions with drug molecules. Nevertheless, it is crucial to conduct an in-depth investigation of the electronic effects on interaction sites.

Due to the lack of a definitive method, such as single crystal XRD, to determine the precise structure of the carbon dot and its interaction with the antiviral drug amantadine, we employed a combination of experimental and theoretical approaches. UV-Vis and FTIR spectroscopies were used to validate the structural characteristics. To thoroughly examine the structural and electronic effects of the interaction between the carbon dots and amantadine, we applied various analytical techniques, including the QTAIM, NBO, IGMplot, and FMO methods. Furthermore, this research provides a comprehensive analysis of the interaction strength between carbon dots and amantadine. A detailed breakdown of the energy components aids in comprehending the nature of these interactions, whereas the energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO offers insights into the stability and reactivity of the CD complex. This research will provide valuable insights for developing a customizable drug delivery system based on carbon dots.

Tribulus terrestris was collected from a local garden and washed with water to remove impurities. The leaves were then separated from the weeds and air-dried prior to use. Finally, the separated leaves were pulverized to obtain tribulus terrestris leaf powder (TTLF) for the preparation of Carbon dot. Amantadine (parkitidin) was obtained from Sun Pharma Laboratories and purchased from a local medical shop in Erode, Tamil Nadu, India.

A solution of AM (10 mg/mL) was mixed with 5 mL of aqueous CD (50 mg/mL of TTLF) synthesized from TTLF. The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature using a magnetic stirrer at 1100 rpm for approximately 1 h. The resulting solution was used for further analysis.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Spectrum two. Absorption spectra were recorded on a JASCO V-750 UV-Vis spectrometer, and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a Horiba Jobin Yvon model FL3-22 Fluorolog spectrofluorimeter. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using an FEI TECNAI G2 S-Twin TEM.

The molecular model of CD used in theoretical calculations was constructed based on experimental observations UV-Vis and FTIR spectrum. Geometry optimization of the CD, AM and AMCD molecules and potential energy curve calculation were carried out using the r2SCAN-3c method [60,61] with a conductor-like polarizable continuum model CPCM (water) in ORCA software [62]. Geometrical optimization was performed without any constraints on the bond length, bond angles, or dihedral angles. All optimized geometries were confirmed to be local minima without any imaginary frequencies in vibrational analysis calculations. Computational analysis of the UV-Vis spectra and electronic transitions was conducted using Time-Dependent Density Functional Theory (TD-DFT). The calculations employed the CAM-B3LYP [63] functional, along with the Def2-TZVP basis set. The resulting electronic transitions were examined by visualizing the Natural Transition Orbitals (NTOs). The aug-cc-pVDZ basis set and auxiliary basis set aug-cc-pVDZ/C aug-cc-pVTZ/JK with RIJK approximation were utilized in the HFLD method to calculate the interaction energy [64]. While HFLD requires slightly more computational resources than the HF method, it consistently performs better than DLPNO-CCSD in accurately computing intermolecular interaction energies. Examination of the interaction energy and energy decomposition analysis (EDA) involves two computational approaches. First, SAPT0 calculations were executed using the jun-cc-pVDZ basis set in the Psi4 [65] software. Additionally, XEDA [66,67] was implemented using the B3LYP-D3BJ/def2-DZVP methodology. The wave functions acquired from the geometry optimization calculations were used to explore the bond topological properties derived using the AIMALL 19.10.12 software [68]. The deformation density contour map of the molecules were drawn using the multiwfn 3.8 software package [69]. The intrinsic bond strength index (IBSI) analysis was performed using the independent gradient model by IGMplot (Rev. 3.08) software using the wave function derived from the M062-X methods with a 6-31G** basis set [70–72]. NCI analysis was performed using NCIPLOT 4.3. The NBO analysis was performed using NBO 7.0.10. Chemcraft package [73] and VMD [74] were utilized to visualize the molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CD and AMCD
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 (a) shows the electronic spectra of CD in water, which were recorded to analyze the optical properties of the CD. The absorption peak starts at 555 nm and two peaks appear at 275 nm and 320 nm, which are due to π →
π
⁎
transition of the conjugated C=C band and n →
π
⁎
transition of C=O band. This result is similar to that in the previous literature [20]. Theoretical UV-Vis spectra were computed with time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) CAM-B3LYP method and Def2-TZVP basis set (with CPCM(water) used to calculate UV-Vis spectra) and compared with experimental results (Fig. 1(a) (red)). To analyze the UV-Vis spectra of CD and AMCD, we calculated the Natural Transition Orbitals (NTOs) of both molecules using their equilibrium structures. Fig. 2 shows that the primary NTOs for CD and AMCD display π →
π
⁎
and n →
π
⁎
transitions. These ground and excited state properties are primarily attributed to the CD ring, with secondary contributions from the lone pairs on CD's functional groups. Furthermore, the figures illustrate that the orbital involvement in the electronic excitation for both molecules is primarily due to transitions between HOMO-LUMO and HOMO-1 -LUMO+1 energy levels. The observed tailing of the UV-Vis spectrum into the visible range ∼ 450–500 nm is attributed to transitions involving localized surface states, which arise from structural imperfections, surface defects, and the presence of various functional groups. These localized states introduce a broad distribution of energy levels, enabling absorption at longer wavelengths beyond the π-
π
⁎
and n-
π
⁎
transitions. Such behavior is consistent with the excitation-dependent photoluminescence observed in the synthesized CD [75].

The fluorescence properties of carbon dots arise from a complex interplay of factors-, including their size, surface states, sp2 domains, and chemical composition. Fig. 3 (a) shows the PL spectra of the aqueous CD solution at different excitation wavelengths, ranging from 350 to 550 nm at intervals of 50 nm. With an increase in the excitation wavelength, the emission peak also shifted to a higher wavelength (red shift) from 502 nm to 590 nm. The emission characteristics of CD vary based on the wavelength used for excitation, which is a distinctive feature of these materials [3,6].

Fig. 3(b) shows an TEM image and size distribution of CD obtained by thermal carbonization at 185 o C, which shows that the CD are largely monodispersed and amorphous in nature. This clearly reveals that the CD were quasi-spherical in shape. This image shows good contrast, without apparent aggregation and a narrow size dispersion ranging from 1 to 8 nm, as shown by the size distribution histogram drawn from the TEM images and an average size of 4 nm. Fig. 4 shows the experimental (Fig. 4a) and theoretical (Fig. 4b) FTIR spectra of the CD, AM, and AMCD. Theoretically derived FTIR spectra were obtained from the optimization and frequency calculation using r2SCAN-3c method. The presence of -NH and carboxylic -OH bonds in CD was confirmed with experimental frequency range of 3628-3300 cm−1 and theoretically at 3568 cm−1 (NH), 3708 cm−1 (carboxylic OH) and 3773 cm−1 (free O-H group). In experimental results, the NH and OH stretching vibrations merged (3628-3300 cm−1) due to strong vibrational coupling, as observed in prior studies [76–78]. The C=C bond was detected experimentally at 1638 cm−1 and theoretically at 1666 cm−1. The C–H bond vibration appeared experimentally at 1162 and 1117 cm−1, aligning with a theoretical value of 1158 cm−1. The presence of hydrophilic groups, including COOH, NH2 and OH, contributes to the excellent water solubility of CD [76]. After interaction with AM drug with CD, the new frequency 2253 cm−1 is observed, which is shifted from 3708 cm−1. This change in frequency is likely involves proton transfer and the formation of a strong hydrogen bond. This interaction leads to the carboxylic acid group in CD donate a proton to the amine group of AM, leading to the formation of a carboxylate anion (-COO−) and a protonated amine (-NH3 +). In both experimental and theoretical FT-IR calculation, 1600-1660 cm−1 peaks represent C=C stretching vibration of aromatic rings. Similarly, the experimental and theoretical observation, the broad peak 3400 to 3700 cm−1 is observed for O-H group. The IR spectrum of AM obtained from experimental results is close to theoretical approach. Moreover, the IR spectrum of CD and AMCD obtained from the DFT method is close to the experimental value. Based on these results, we proceed DFT calculations for further analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural evaluation of CD, AM and AMCD
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular structure of amantadine was optimized using r2SCAN-3c method. The geometric parameters of AM (Table S1) closely matched the experimental data (CCDC No:1913280) [79]. This strong correlation between the computational and experimental data confirms the reliability of the r2SCAN-3c method for predicting molecular geometries and molecular electronic properties [61].

Fig. 5 shows the energy minimized structure of the AM, CD and AMCD. Table 1 lists the selected geometrical parameters of the CD, AM and AMCD. Tables S2 and S3 show the detailed geometrical parameters of CD and AMCD. In AMCD, the O36-H42 bond length increases from 0.9735 Å to 1.5536 Å, with its bond order decreasing from 0.8442 to 0.6469, due to the formation of a new N59-H42 bond between CD and AM at a bond length of 1.0926 Å and a bond order of 0.2181. These changes indicate a hydrogen bond with proton transfer between O36-H42 and N59 and weakening of the O36-H42 bond. The newly formed N59-H42 bond suggests a non-covalent interaction that stabilizes the system through intermolecular forces. A decrease in bond length was observed in the C33-O36 bond, from 1.3479 Å to 1.2775 Å, along with an increase in bond order from 1.0674 to 1.1634. Similarly, the C33-O35 bond shows a slight increase in bond length by 0.03 Å and a decrease in bond order by 0.101. These differences in bond length and bond order suggest the delocalization of electron through O35-C33-036. Upon interaction with AM, the bond angle of O36-C33-O35 in CD increased from 122.42∘ to 125.87∘. This change arises from shifts in electron density around C33 owing to lone-pair repulsions on O36 and O35, resulting in a wider angle. The bond angle of H28-N1-H8 in AM increases from 105∘ to 107.11∘ after interacting with the CD molecule. This change is likely due to the changes in electron density around the nitrogen atom and potential steric interactions, which can alter the electron pair repulsion on N1 and slightly widen the H28-N1-H8 bond angle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction energies and energy decomposition analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The Hartree-Fock method with London dispersion corrections (HFLD) is a first-principles technique for evaluating and examining non-covalent interactions (NCIs) in large molecular systems [64]. This approach is based on domain-based local pair natural orbital coupled-cluster (DLPNO-CC) theory and offers an ab initio solution for studying these interactions. LED methodology was subsequently utilized to isolate the London dispersion (LD) energy from the intermolecular correlation component. The extracted LD energy was then used to modify the interaction energies determined at the HF level. As a result, HFLD can be characterized as a dispersion-augmented HF approach, in which the dispersion interaction between fragments is integrated at the DLPNO-CC level. Consequently, this method demonstrates exceptional accuracy in quantifying non-covalent interactions, particularly those prevalent in hydrogen-bonded systems. The LED scheme is then used to single out the
E
disp
C-CCSD
component of the interaction energy, which was then simply added to the HF interaction energy.
(1)
Δ
E
int
HFLD
= Δ
E
int
HF
+
E
disp
C-CCSD
The HFLD method, along with the aug-cc-pVDZ basis set and RIJK approximation, was employed to calculate the interaction energy for AMCD. The resulting interaction energy from the HFLD method was determined to be -88.775 kcal/mol. This value closely aligns with the interaction energy obtained using the SAPT0 method in the psi4 code, which yielded -89.569 kcal/mol.

EDA provides the total interaction energy for individual components, such as electrostatics, exchange, repulsion, polarization, and dispersion. This detailed breakdown provides a deeper understanding of the forces driving molecular interactions. We performed two types of energy decomposition analyses such as SAPT0 and XEDA (Fig. 6 ). Among the negative contributions, electrostatic and dispersion components have a more positive contribution in the stability of molecules.

The SAPT approach allows us to identify the physical phenomena underlying the interaction energy. The observed charge transfer (CT) value of -34.139 kcal/mol was considerably high, suggesting an effective interaction between the donor and acceptor molecules. Molecular arrangements that enhance orbital overlap are likely to yield higher CT values. Such configurations can exert notable influences on the stability and characteristics of the molecular AMCD complex. Observed interaction components from a subtle equilibrium between attractive and repulsive forces. Exchange interaction contributes a substantial repulsive element (111.65 kcal/mol), while electrostatic (-70.66 kcal/mol), induction (-113.32 kcal/mol), and dispersion (-17.24 kcal/mol) forces act attractively, resulting in an overall stabilizing effect. The stability of AMCD complex is primarily due to strong induction and electrostatic attractions, which overcome exchange repulsion. This indicates a configuration in which the molecules are optimally positioned to enhance these attractive interactions. Additionally, an alternative Energy Decomposition Analysis (EDA) technique, known as XEDA, was employed using the DFT method. The analysis revealed that attractive forces, including electrostatic (-73.23 k cal), exchange (-162.18 k cal), Polarization (-159.48 kcal), and Grimme dispersion correction (-6.22 k cal), were counterbalanced by electron correlation (16.62 k cal) and repulsion energy (312.06 k cal). The resulting net interaction energy was calculated to be (-72.43 k cal), which aligns with the findings from other methodologies. The enhanced stability observed in the AMCD complexes can be attributed to hydrogen transfer from CD to AM.

PES provides a detailed analysis of proton motion in intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The study of PES contributes to a deeper understanding of hydrogen bond properties. The PES was calculated adiabatically that is all atoms in the system were kept fixed except for the proton in the hydrogen bridge. Fig. 7 representing the potential energy curve for the intermolecular hydrogen bond (N–H–O) in AMCD showed a well-shaped second minimum, which was deeper than the minimum for the molecular form of the complex. This indicates a high degree of proton transfer and the presence of an ionic form of the complex. Second minimum (representing the ionic form) is deeper than the first minimum (representing the molecular form). This indicates that the ionic form is more stable than the molecular form of AMCD complex. This is consistent with CD being an acid and AM being a base and PES curve provides a detailed understanding of the hydrogen bond stability and the transition pathway between the CD and the drug molecule. An animation video of the proton transfer potential energy surface was generated using IBOview for intrinsic bond orbital (IBO) calculations (refer to Supporting Information). This visualization demonstrates that the hydrogen bonding and salt formation are attributed to the non-bonding electrons present on the nitrogen and oxygen atoms.

Bader's Atoms in Molecules (AIM) theory presents a systematic approach to comprehending chemical bonding through the analysis of electron density distribution topology within molecular structures. This methodology enables the identification and evaluation of critical points, yielding detailed insights into the nature and strength of the hydrogen-bonding interactions observed in AM and CD molecules. Several topological parameters can be used to describe the bonding characteristics between the atoms. These includes, electron density ρ(r), the Laplacian of electron density
∇
2
ρ (r), Eigenvalues ( λ 1 , λ 2 , λ 3 ), and ellipticity (ϵ) among others, at the bond critical point. The distribution of the electronic charge density and electrostatic properties of the AMCD can be examined. Using the wave function derived from the r2SCAN-3c method, the topological parameters at the bond critical point for all bonds in the AM, CD, and AMCD were computed. These results are presented in tables S4-S6, the selected topological parameters are highlighted in Table 2 . A molecular graph with the critical point and bond path (Fig. 8 (a)) contour map of Laplacian of electron density (Fig. 8(b)) is shown.

Most of the inner parts of the CD had similar electron densities after the formation AMCD complex (Figure S1(a)). This indicated that the transfer of protons from CD to AM did not significantly affect the remaining structure of the molecule (CD and AM). The N59-H57 and N59-H66 bonds in AMCD exhibited higher electron densities (0.3338 eBohr−3 and 0.3339 eBohr−3, respectively) than the newly formed N59-H42 bond (0.2699 eBohr−3). The lower electron density of N59-H42 indicates a weaker bonding characteristic relative to the other two N-H bonds examined.

The Laplacian of electron density is a mathematical function used to analyze the nature and formation regions of chemical bonds, as well as to identify locations where lone pair electrons are present. The Laplacian of electron density analysis is renowned from AIM theory. It is a three-dimensional real-space function expressed explicitly as follows: [80,81]

represents the Laplacian operator, ρ corresponds to electron density, r is position vector, and x, y, z are its Cartesian components.

The Laplacian of the electron density image for AMCD is displayed in Fig. 8(b), whereas Figure S2 presents the same for AM and CD. The Laplacian of electron density offers insights into the areas of localized charge accumulation or depletion within molecules. This mathematical technique, based on atoms-in-molecules (AIM) theory, is crucial for understanding molecular structures by examining the chemical bond characteristics and formation sites. The deformation density (ρdef) is the difference between the actual electron density of the molecules and that of all their constituent atoms in the free state [85]. This technique is used to study the electron density changes in the molecule during interaction. The deformation density can be expressed by [85,86]

Fig. 9 shows the contour map of the (ρdef) calculated and imaged from the wavefunction of the r2SCAN-3c method for AMCD molecules. This image reveals that hydrogen bonding between N59 and H42 causes an accumulation of electron density, as represented by the red color. The O36-H42 bond in CD, as shown in Figure S3a, showed a high concentration of electron density. However, after interaction with the NH2 group of AM, as depicted in Figure S3b, this bond undergoes a noticeable redistribution of electron density. The lower electron density along the O36-H42 bond suggests interaction with the -NH2 group. This redistribution causes the oxygen atom to pull more electrons toward itself, slightly increasing its electron density. These changes provide insight into how hydrogen bonding can significantly alter the electron dynamics in molecular structures. Similarly, the nitrogen atom in AM (Figure S3c) exhibited a modest electron distribution. After the interaction (Figure S3d), significantly altered electron accumulation along O-H-N, likely due to hydrogen transfer and hydrogen bonding.

The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) provides valuable information related to the distribution of electronic charges in a molecule. This helps to predict regions of high and low electron densities in a molecule. The nuclei and electrons of a molecule create an electrostatic potential V(r) at each point r in the surrounding space. The electrostatic potential, V(r), is expressed by the equation

The electrostatic potential (ESP) isosurface of AMCD, calculated using the r2SCAN-3c method, is depicted in Fig. 10 . The visualization includes both the positive (blue) and negative (red) ESP regions. Figures S4a and S4b illustrate the ESP isosurfaces for the isolated AM and CDstructures, respectively. In Figure S4a, the ESP of AM exhibits negative potential areas corresponding to the lone electron pairs on N1. The ESP of CD, as shown in Figure S4b, demonstrates a positive MEP on H39, H40, H41, and H42, indicating the electrophilic nature of the molecule. Among the four hydrogen atoms, H42 is particularly vulnerable to nucleophilic attack. These studies revealed the formation of proton transferred hydrogen bonds between CD and AM molecule through N-H-O atoms.

Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) analysis is a powerful theoretical tool for understanding electronic structure and bonding interactions. By transforming canonical molecular orbitals into a set of localized bond orbitals, NBO analysis provides detailed insights into covalent bonds, lone pairs, and hyperconjugative interactions [87,88].

The stabilization energy, E(2), associated with the donor and acceptor orbitals, is determined using second-order perturbation theory, expressed as:
(5)

represents the energy of the donor orbital.

Table 3 shows the hyperbond characteristics in the NBO of AMCD studied. In NBO hyperbond analysis, each A:-B-:C triad (3-center, 4-electron hyperbonds) denotes strong nC → σAB* delocalization in the A-B:C Lewis structure or, equivalently, strong nA → σBC* delocalization in the alternative A:B-C Lewis structure. In AMCD molecule three (C11:- C8-: C9, C15:- C10-: C9, O43:- C32-: N34) sets of 3-center, 4-electron hyperbond were observed. Among these hyperbonds, two bonds (C11:- C8-: C9 & C15:- C10-: C9) were located inside the CD structure. Out of the two hyperbond, the C11:- C8-: C9 hyperbond has a higher C11-C8 bonding character (51.5%) than C15-C10 (51%) in the C15:- C10-: C9 hyperbond. This suggests that the delocalized electron density over the C11:- C8-: C9 hyperbond results in a lower electron density on C9 than C8 in the C15:- C10-: C9 hyperbond. The rest of the one (O43:- C32-: N34) is located at the side of the CD in the amide functional group, and it delocalizes the electron between O43-C32 and the lone pair of electrons in the N34 atom. The occupancy value of the hyperbond present in the outer side of CD was higher (3.9875) than that of the hyperbond present inside the structure (3.0319, 3.0607), because of the extended conjugation within the CD and planarity of the CD.

The results in Table 4 represent the donor-acceptor interactions and second-order perturbation energies E(2) for CD, AM and AMCD calculated using the r2SCAN-3c method. In AMCD, n1 electron of N59 conjugated with the LV1 electron of H42 results 350.94 kcal/mol for E(2). Similarly, the decrease in occupancy from 1.9729e to 1.9586 e for the n1 electrons of O36 conjugated with the LV1 electron of H42. It can also decrease the E(2) value from 13.41 kcal/mol to 11.11 kcal/mol. The E(2) value of N59-H42 is higher compared with E(2) value O36-H42, which results N-H bond is stronger than O-H bond. The selected NBO orbital is illustrated in Fig. 11 .

The Independent Gradient Model (IGM) Plot provides a detailed visualization and understanding of non-covalent interactions in molecular systems. The IGMPlot highlights regions of significant electron density gradients, revealing the presence and strength of hydrogen bonds within and between molecules. This combined approach helps to elucidate the effects of hydrogen bonding on molecular stability, reactivity, and electronic structure, enhancing insights into various applications, such as drug design and supramolecular material synthesis.

The IGM method involves calculating the electron density gradients in a molecule and comparing them to the gradients of a reference non-interacting system. The key equation for this comparison is as follows,
(6)

The difference in the gradient norm at a specific location r is denoted by IGM ( r ) , ∇

interacting
( r ) is the gradient of the electron density in the interacting system, ∇
ρ
reference
( r ) is the gradient of the electron density in the non-interacting reference system.

By visualizing the difference in gradients, the IGM plot highlights regions where non-covalent interactions are significant, thereby providing insights into the spatial distribution and strength of these interactions within a molecule. This mathematical framework and graphical representation make the IGM plot a powerful tool for studying weak interactions in complex molecular systems, aiding in understanding the underlying electronic structure and interactions that govern the molecular behavior. The intrinsic bond strength index (IBSI) is a valuable tool that, quantifies the relative strength of the chemical bonds in molecules. This provides a numerical value that indicates the inherent stability or strength of a bond within a specific molecule. IBSI was expressed as [72]

The IBSI values were calculated using the wave function derived from the M062-X/6-31G** density functional method and are tabulated in Table 5 for the CDs with AM. To compare the bond strength and direction of bond asymmetry, the selected IBSI, PDA, and direction of asymmetry values were tabulated.

The IBSI value of the O36-H42 bond in CD is 1.367. After interaction with the AM molecule, the IBSI value of the O36-H42 is decreased from 1.367 to 0.258, which clearly indicates that the strength of the O-H bond decreased. During this interaction, the N59 atoms of the drug molecule interact with the H42 atom of CD, thereby forming N-H bond in the AMCD molecule. The IBSI value of newly formed H42-N59 covalent bond is 0.795 (Table 5). From this value, it is evident that H42-N59 is a stronger bond than the O36-H42 bond in the AMCD molecule.

For intermolecular interaction and quantification of the interaction arising from the partitioning of electron densities between atoms in the AMCD molecule, the IGM- δg descriptor approach was plotted and visualized using the VMD program. Fig. 12 depicts the plots of δg vs. the sign ( λ 2 ) ρ values of the AMCD. These values were used to evaluate the attractive and repulsive interactions in the AMCD. In the AMCD molecule, the λ2 value (Table 3) for N-H and O-H interactions were negative. In Fig. 12, δg of the N-H interaction is higher than that of O-H interaction. It is evident that, the N-H bond is stronger than O-H bond. A greater δg value for the N-H bond indicates a higher level of electron density interpenetration and sharing between the atoms involved in N-H bond than O-H bond. Therefore, a bond with a greater δg pair value may suggest a stronger interaction between atoms, reflecting a more stable and potentially stronger bond in the molecule.

The Non-Covalent Interaction (NCI) index is a powerful tool for visualizing weak intermolecular forces, particularly through the use of the reduced density gradient (RDG). The RDG plot of AMCD are shown in Figure S5. The RDG is defined as: [89]

where ρ(r) is the electron density and ∇ρ(r) is its gradient. In this study, RDG analysis was used to investigate the non-covalent interactions within the AMCD complex. The RDG scatter plot of S versus sign (
λ

) ρ provides insight into the type and strength of interactions. Green spikes near sign (
λ

) ρ ≈ 0 are characteristic of van der Waals forces, while more negative or positive values correspond to attractive or repulsive interactions, respectively. For the AMCD system, the presence of green spikes confirms the dominance of van der Waals and electrostatic interactions between the CD and the drug. These interactions contribute significantly to the stability of the complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frontier molecular orbital and TDOS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen bonds can significantly affect the HOMO-LUMO gap by altering the electronic environment of a molecule. When a hydrogen bond forms, it can induce a redistribution of the electron density, which can modify the energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO. Typically, the formation of a hydrogen bond stabilizes the HOMO, potentially lowering its energy, and influencing the LUMO. FMO helps to analyze the interaction of CD with AM in connection with the HOMO-LUMO band gap. The HOMO and LUMO of the CD before and after interaction with AM are shown in Fig. 13 .

In the interaction of AM molecule with the CD, energy between the HOMO and LUMO levels is increased relative to the pure carbon dot, which shows the greatest increase in the interaction of the CD and AM, which is caused by the molecular energy absorption matched from this position. The carbon dot has a HOMO-LUMO band gap (Eg) of 2.2863 eV, where the HOMO is -4.9841 eV, and the LUMO is -2.6977 eV. After interaction of AM with CD, the HOMO and LUMO of the system decreased to -4.8774, -2.4079 respectively. An increase in the HOMO and LUMO energy levels might indicate a stronger interaction between the carbon dot and the drug molecule. This could potentially enhance the binding of the AM to CD, which is desirable for controlled drug delivery. These studies provide the insight of tunable drug delivery systems by changing the electronic effect of CD. The Total density of states (TDOS) helps to understand the electronic properties of the geometries. The observed TDOS spectra of the AMCD (Figure S6) justify the HOMO and LUMO representation of AMCD.

We validated the structure of CD and AMCD using UV-Vis and FTIR spectra derived from DFT calculation and experimental spectra and TEM. The interaction between the carbon dots and amantadine was thoroughly examined. Highly accurate and reliable HFLD, SAPT0 and XEDA were applied, and the high value of charge transfer and interaction energy were observed owing to the major contribution of the attractive components. The hydrogen transfer pathway consisted of two energy minima with the deepest one confirming complete hydrogen transfer and CD and AM salt formation. This method can be used to design tunable cocrystal formations for biological applications. The electron density and deformation density of this system provides a clear picture of interacting sites and bonding nature. The intrinsic bond strength and increasing HOMO-LUMO gap indicate that the N-H interaction is stronger than O-H interactions. Both the interactions are due to the n electrons of nitrogen and oxygen interacting with the hydrogen loan vacancy. This research will provide valuable insights for developing a customizable drug delivery system based on carbon dots.

Karthik Krishnasamy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Thangavel Subramani: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization.
